howItWorks.intro: A Morgan or Peace dollar is 90% silver and 10% copper, which works out to 0.7734 troy ounces of actual silver weight (ASW) per coin. This calculator multiplies that fixed silver content by the spot price you enter and by how many coins you hold.
rules: Melt Value = Silver Content (oz) × Spot Price × Quantity
howItWorks.outro: Melt value is the raw metal floor. A worn common-date Morgan often trades right around melt, while a scarce date or a high-grade coin sells for well above it — so treat this number as your baseline, not the ceiling.

faq.question: How much silver is in a Morgan or Peace dollar?
faq.answer: Both hold 0.7734 troy ounces of pure silver (about 24.06 grams). They weigh 26.73 grams total at 90% fineness; the remaining 10% is copper, which has no meaningful bullion value.

faq.question: How do I calculate melt value by hand?
faq.answer: Multiply 0.7734 oz by the current silver spot price. At $30/oz, one Morgan dollar is worth about 0.7734 × 30 = $23.20 in silver. A roll of 20 would be roughly $464 in melt.

faq.question: What's the difference between melt value and collector value?
faq.answer: Melt value is what the silver alone is worth if the coin were refined. Collector (numismatic) value comes from date, mint mark, and condition. An 1893-S Morgan or a well-preserved key date can be worth hundreds or thousands over melt, so check the date before you sell to a scrap buyer.

faq.question: Do all silver dollars contain 90% silver?
faq.answer: No. Morgan (1878-1921) and Peace (1921-1935) dollars are 90% silver. Eisenhower dollars struck for circulation (1971-1978) are copper-nickel clad with no silver. Only the special-issue 40% silver Eisenhower dollars from 1971-1976 contain any silver — about 0.3161 oz ASW each.

faq.question: Which US dollar coins have no silver at all?
faq.answer: Circulating Eisenhower dollars, Susan B. Anthony dollars, Sacagawea dollars, and Presidential dollars contain zero silver. If you're valuing an old jar of dollar coins, only the pre-1936 Morgans and Peace dollars (and the 40% Ike collector coins) carry silver melt value.

faq.question: Should I sell my silver dollars for melt?
faq.answer: Only after checking dates. Common, circulated Morgans and Peace dollars usually sell for a modest premium over melt, so a bullion buyer paying spot-based melt is reasonable. But pull out any key dates, carson City (CC) mint marks, or lustrous uncirculated coins first — those belong with a coin dealer, not a melt buyer.

howWeCalculate.intro: The math is fixed silver content times live spot. The only variable you truly need to get right is which coin you have, because that sets the actual silver weight.
steps: Pick your coin type — Morgan and Peace dollars use 0.7734 oz ASW; a 40% Eisenhower uses 0.3161 oz; a Silver Eagle is a full 1.0 troy oz.
Enter today's silver spot price in dollars per troy ounce (you can pull it from any live quote).
Enter how many coins you have to get a total for the whole lot.
The calculator multiplies silver weight by spot by quantity and shows melt per coin plus the roll or bag total — all in your browser, nothing sent to a server.
howWeCalculate.disclaimer: This gives you the intrinsic silver value only. Dealers buy below melt and sell above it, and collector coins can be worth far more, so use this figure as a reference point, not a firm offer.

whenToUse.intro: Reach for this whenever a silver dollar is really a lump of silver to you rather than a collectible — and to know the floor before anyone quotes you a price.
scenarios: Selling coins to a dealer or scrap buyer and wanting to know the metal floor before you accept an offer.
Valuing an inherited coin jar or estate lot where most pieces are common-date circulated dollars.
Stacking silver and tracking how much actual silver weight your Morgan and Peace dollars add to your holdings.
Deciding whether a coin is worth grading — if a date is common and worn, melt is likely all it's worth.
Sanity-checking a pawn shop or eBay price against the spot-based silver value.

sections.title: What This Calculator Tells You
sections.body: Every Morgan (1878-1921) and Peace (1921-1935) dollar is struck from 90% silver, giving it 0.7734 troy ounces of actual silver weight. This tool takes that fixed weight, multiplies it by the silver spot price you enter, and multiplies again by how many coins you have, so you get both the per-coin melt and the total for a roll, bag, or inherited jar. It works for 40% silver Eisenhower dollars (0.3161 oz) and one-ounce Silver Eagles too.

sections.title: A Worked Example
sections.body: Say silver is trading at $30 per ounce and you have a single Morgan dollar. Its 0.7734 oz of silver is worth 0.7734 × 30, or about $23.20 in melt — before any collector premium. A full roll of 20 Morgans holds 15.468 oz and melts to roughly $464 at that same spot price. Change the spot price and the number scales straight in line with it, since the silver content per coin never changes.

sections.title: Melt Is the Floor, Not the Ceiling
sections.body: Melt value assumes the coin is nothing but its metal. That's the right frame for a worn, common-date dollar, but plenty of silver dollars are worth more. Key dates, Carson City mint marks, and coins with original luster carry numismatic value that can dwarf melt — an 1889-CC Morgan runs into the thousands regardless of the silver price. Check the date and mint mark before you treat any dollar as scrap.

howToSteps.title: Choose the Coin Type
howToSteps.description: Select Morgan, Peace, 40% Eisenhower, or Silver Eagle. This sets the silver weight — 0.7734 oz for Morgan and Peace, 0.3161 oz for a 40% Ike, and a full ounce for an Eagle.

howToSteps.title: Enter the Number of Coins
howToSteps.description: Count the coins of that type you're valuing. For a standard roll of dollars that's 20; for a $1,000 face bag of 90% silver dollars it's 1,000 coins.

howToSteps.title: Enter the Silver Spot Price
howToSteps.description: Type the current silver price in dollars per troy ounce. Spot moves all day, so use a live quote for an accurate melt figure.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Run the numbers. You'll see silver per coin, melt per coin, total silver ounces, and total melt value instantly.

howToSteps.title: Read the Results Against Real Offers
howToSteps.description: Compare the total melt to any dealer or buyer offer. Common coins near melt are fine to sell; if a date looks scarce, price it as a collectible before letting it go.

tips: Confirm the coin is actually 90% silver — a circulating Eisenhower dollar looks similar but contains no silver, so it has zero melt value.
Only the 1971-1976 special-issue 40% silver Eisenhower dollars carry silver; the regular clad Ikes do not, so don't lump them together.
Weigh a genuine 90% dollar and expect about 26.73 grams total; a lighter or magnetic coin is a red flag for a counterfeit.
For a full $1,000 face-value bag of silver dollars, expect roughly 773 troy ounces of silver, since each dollar holds 0.7734 oz.
Pull key dates and Carson City (CC) coins out of any lot before melting — their collector value can be many times the silver price.
